nameMeanings.name: Gervase
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Angelo, Rocco, Salvatore.
Americanized form of Italian   Gervasio  .
English: variant of   Jarvis  . This surname is now extinct in Britain.

variants.name: Gervasio
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Emilio, Aldo, Angelo, Antonio, Dino, Egidio, Eluterio, Francesca, Gregorio, Raffaele, Ugo.  Spanish Fidel, Jose, Manuel, Maurilio, Romana.
Italian and Spanish (mainly Biscay): from the personal name Gervasio (see Jarvis ).

nameMeanings.name: of
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Chinese: Cheng, Jing, Kam Fung, Wai Shing, Wing, Yali, Yuk.
Chinese:
variant Romanization of the surnames 歐 and 區, see   Ou   1 and 2.

variants.name: Ou
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Chinese Wei, Ying, Ching, Heng, Hong, Jia, Kuang Yu, Li-Ching, Cho, Chong, Chung, Sa.  Southeast Asian Lichun, Ming, Quan, Roeun, Sokhom, Chau, Hai, Leang, Ly, Nhanh, Phan, Pho, Tha.

Chinese: Mandarin form of the surname 歐, meaning ‘to sing someone's praise’ in ancient Chinese: (i) from the placename Dong Ou (東甌), a fief (in present-day Wenzhou in Zhejiang province) which was granted to Yao in 192  BC . It is said that Yao was a descendant of Wujiang (reigned 355–333  BC ), king of the state of Yue (located mainly in present-day Zhejiang and Jiangxi provinces) during the Spring and Autumn period (770–476  BC ). (ii) from the placename Ou Yu (歐餘), the name of a fief (located in present-day Zhejiang province) that was granted to Ti, the second son of Wujiang (reigned 355–333  BC ), king of the state of Yue (located mainly in present-day Zhejiang and Jiangxi provinces) during the Spring and Autumn period (770–476  BC ).

Chinese: Mandarin form of the surname 區, originally denoting an ancient vessel for measurement in ancient Chinese: from the personal name of Ou Zhizi (歐治子), an expert sword-maker who lived during the Spring and Autumn period (770–476 BC ). Some of his descendants inherited Ou (歐) as their surname, simplifying the Chinese character 歐 to 區.

nameMeanings.name: Eskdale
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of Family Names in Britain and Ireland © University of the West of England 2016
meanings: 1  Scottish: locative name from Eskdale in Dumfriess.
2  English: relationship name in NR Yorks a variant of   Eskell  , altered by false association with the local place-name Eskdale; see (3).
3  English: locative name perhaps from Eskdale, near Whitby in NR Yorks, attested in the 1301 example, or possibly Eskdale in Cumb, but there is no evidence of either producing a hereditary surname.

variants.name: Easdale
meanings: Scottish: (i) locative name, most probably from Eskdale (Dumfriess), see Eskdale . Variant spellings without -k- are recorded in Dumfriess from an early date. (ii) locative name, it is theoretically possible, but unlikely, that this name comes from Easdale (Argyll).

variants.name: Eskell
meanings: English: relationship name from Middle English Eskill (from Old Scandinavian, Danish dialect), a variant of Askell . The preposition in the 1301 example from Silpho is probably a scribal error. The surname was early altered by folk etymology to Est(h)ill, later to Easthill, Easthall, Esdale, and Eskdale.

nameMeanings.name: Sybilla
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: From the name (Greek Sibylla or Sybilla, with confusion over the vowels from an early period) of a class of ancient prophetesses inspired by Apollo. According to medieval theology, they were pagans denied the knowledge of Christ but blessed by God with some insight into things to come and accordingly admitted to heaven. It was thus regarded as a respectable name to be borne by Christians. This name was introduced to England by the Normans and enjoyed considerable popularity in the Middle Ages.

variants.name: Sibilla
meanings: From the name (Greek Sibylla or Sybilla, with confusion over the vowels from an early period) of a class of ancient prophetesses inspired by Apollo. According to medieval theology, they were pagans denied the knowledge of Christ but blessed by God with some insight into things to come and accordingly admitted to heaven. It was thus regarded as a respectable name to be borne by Christians. This name was introduced to England by the Normans and enjoyed considerable popularity in the Middle Ages.

variants.name: Sybille
meanings: From Greek. See Sybil .

variants.name: Sibella
meanings: From the name (Greek Sibylla or Sybilla, with confusion over the vowels from an early period) of a class of ancient prophetesses inspired by Apollo. According to medieval theology, they were pagans denied the knowledge of Christ but blessed by God with some insight into things to come and accordingly admitted to heaven. It was thus regarded as a respectable name to be borne by Christians. This name was introduced to England by the Normans and enjoyed considerable popularity in the Middle Ages.

en.lifeSummary: Sybilla Gervase of Eskdale was born about 1162, in Europe. She had at least 4 sons and 1 daughter with Gervase d'Avenel.
